title: Equivalent equations
source: Essential Mathematics for the Australian Curriculum, Year 7 (Cambridge, Greenwood et al. 2024) — Chapter 9 Equations, Section 9C. Images are direct crops of the original textbook pages.

questions.topic: matching an equation to its equivalent equation
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): C

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: matching an equation to one that merely looks similar rather than checking it's reachable by a genuine single operation
misconceptions.reason: two equations are equivalent only if one can be produced from the other by adding/subtracting/multiplying/dividing both sides by the same amount, or swapping sides - superficial resemblance isn't enough

questions.topic: applying a stated operation to both sides and simplifying
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): 3 + x = 7 + 3, which simplifies to 6 + x = 10

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: using the opposite operation from the one stated, or applying it backwards
misconceptions.reason: the question asks for the result of a SPECIFIC stated operation (e.g. '[+ 3]') applied to both sides - swap it for the wrong operation and you get a different (still possibly true, but not the asked-for) equation

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: a sign error when adding/subtracting a term, e.g. writing 3x = 12 as 3x = 14 - 2 instead of 3x = 14 - 2 correctly
misconceptions.reason: when isolating a term, careless arithmetic with the added/subtracted constant is the most common source of an incorrect equivalent equation

questions.topic: completing an arrow-chain of equivalent equations
questions.answer: 3x + 2 = 14 → (−2 both sides) → 3x = 12 → (÷3 both sides) → x = 4 → (×10 both sides) → 10x = 40 → (+1 both sides) → 10x + 1 = 41
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): 3x + 2 = 14 → (−2 both sides) → 3x = 12 → (÷3 both sides) → x = 4 → (×10 both sides) → 10x = 40 → (+1 both sides) → 10x + 1 = 41

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: applying the operation to only one side of the equation

misconceptions.reason: when isolating a term, careless arithmetic with the added/subtracted constant is the most common source of an incorrect equivalent equation

questions.topic: showing two equations are equivalent via an intermediate equation
questions.answer: 5x − 3 = 32 → (+3 both sides) → 5x = 35 → (÷5 both sides) → x = 7 → (+2 both sides) → x + 2 = 9
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): 5x − 3 = 32 → (+3 both sides) → 5x = 35 → (÷5 both sides) → x = 7 → (+2 both sides) → x + 2 = 9

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: applying the operation to only one side of the equation
misconceptions.reason: an equation stays equivalent (still true for the same solution) only if you do the exact same thing to both sides - changing just one side breaks the balance

questions.topic: showing two equations are equivalent via an intermediate equation
questions.answer: x ÷ 2 = 5 → (×2 both sides) → x = 10 → (+8 both sides) → x + 8 = 18, which is equivalent to (x + 8) ÷ 2 = 9 and to (x ÷ 2) + 4 = 9 (all forced by x = 10)
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): x ÷ 2 = 5 → (×2 both sides) → x = 10 → (+8 both sides) → x + 8 = 18, which is equivalent to (x + 8) ÷ 2 = 9 and to (x ÷ 2) + 4 = 9 (all forced by x = 10)

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: applying the operation to only one side of the equation
misconceptions.reason: an equation stays equivalent (still true for the same solution) only if you do the exact same thing to both sides - changing just one side breaks the balance

questions.topic: multiplying both sides of an equation by zero
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): 0 = 0

questions.topic: multiplying both sides of an equation by zero
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): 0 = 0

questions.topic: explaining why multiplying by zero doesn't give a useful equivalent equation
questions.answer: Regardless of the original equation, multiplying both sides by zero will always result in 0 = 0, so it destroys any information about the original equation.
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): Regardless of the original equation, multiplying both sides by zero will always result in 0 = 0, so it destroys any information about the original equation.

questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): x = 3 → (×7 both sides) → 7x = 21 → (+2 both sides) → 7x + 2 = 23

misconceptions.reason: an equation stays equivalent (still true for the same solution) only if you do the exact same thing to both sides - changing just one side breaks the balance

questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): x = 3 → (×2 both sides) → 2x = 6 → (+8 both sides) → 8 + 2x = 14

misconceptions.reason: an equation stays equivalent (still true for the same solution) only if you do the exact same thing to both sides - changing just one side breaks the balance

questions.topic: classifying general statements about equivalence as true or false
questions.answer: True; you can add 3 to both sides and then subtract 3 to get the original equation again.
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): True; you can add 3 to both sides and then subtract 3 to get the original equation again.

questions.topic: classifying general statements about equivalence as true or false
questions.answer: True; simply perform the opposite operations in the reverse order, so + 4 becomes − 4.
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): True; simply perform the opposite operations in the reverse order, so + 4 becomes − 4.

questions.topic: classifying general statements about equivalence as true or false
questions.answer: True; use the operations that take equation 1 to equation 2, then the operations that take equation 2 to equation 3.
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): True; use the operations that take equation 1 to equation 2, then the operations that take equation 2 to equation 3.

questions.topic: classifying general statements about equivalence as true or false
questions.answer: False; e.g. equation 1: x = 4, equation 2: x = 5, equation 3: 2x = 8 — equation 1 and equation 3 are not equivalent even though neither adjacent pair is.
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): False; e.g. equation 1: x = 4, equation 2: x = 5, equation 3: 2x = 8 — equation 1 and equation 3 are not equivalent even though neither adjacent pair is.
